paper_metadata.title: What explains public support for climate policies? A review of empirical and experimental studies
paper_metadata.authors: Stefan Drews & Jeroen C.J.M. van den Bergh
paper_metadata.geographic_scope: Mostly North America, Australia, and Europe
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Review of 46 core Web of Science studies plus additional papers from citations; includes surveys, choice experiments, and other experiments on public support/WTP
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Synthesizes determinants of public acceptability/support for carbon/CO2 and fuel taxes, focusing on perceived effectiveness, costs, fairness, trust/norms, and design features like revenue earmarking/recycling and tax-label framing.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: people approved ... subsidies ... or expansion of public transportation. To a significantly lesser degree they supported two types of CO2 taxation
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Prefer pull measures over coercive CO2 taxation
determinants.definition: CO2 taxes (push measures) are less supported when compared with non-coercive pull alternatives such as subsidies and expanded public transport.
determinants.mechanism: Coercive taxes are perceived as more costly and freedom-restricting, lowering willingness to support CO2 taxation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss evidence comparing pull measures (subsidies/public transport) vs two CO2-tax types (push) (Tobler et al., 2012).
determinants.evidence_quote: subsidies... public transportation... To a significantly lesser degree they supported two types of CO2 taxation
determinants.notes: Lower acceptability for tax-based push measures vs pull alternatives.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: were more likely to support a fuel tax if they believed the tax was going to reduce driving and exert a positive effect on climate change
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived fuel-tax effectiveness (reduces driving; helps climate)
determinants.definition: Fuel-tax support increases when citizens believe the tax reduces driving and improves climate outcomes.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived effectiveness/benefits make the tax more likely to achieve the intended behavioural change and climate mitigation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian sample: support for a fuel tax conditional on beliefs that it reduces driving and benefits climate (Kallbekken & Sælen, 2011).
determinants.evidence_quote: more likely to support a fuel tax if they believed... reduce driving... and exert a positive effect on climate change
determinants.page_or_section: 3.2. Perceived benefits and effectiveness of policy

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Perceived effectiveness best predicted people’s policy choices... more effective... than a carbon tax
determinants.definition: When carbon-related policies are perceived as more effective, citizens choose/support them more, including carbon taxes.
determinants.mechanism: Policy choices track perceived efficacy; carbon-tax support rises when the tax is viewed as effective versus alternatives.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: International survey comparing general environmental, carbon, and geoengineering policies (Bostrom et al., 2012).
determinants.evidence_quote: Perceived effectiveness best predicted people’s policy choices... more effective... than a carbon tax
determinants.page_or_section: 3.2. Perceived benefits and effectiveness of policy
determinants.notes: Effect is relative (effectiveness vs alternatives including carbon tax).

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: overestimate the costs of taxes and underestimate the ‘hidden’ costs of alternative policies
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Cost misperceptions about taxes reduce support
determinants.definition: Overestimating the costs of taxes (and underestimating hidden costs of alternatives) lowers support for tax-based mitigation.
determinants.mechanism: Biased beliefs inflate perceived personal burden of carbon/fuel taxes and make substitutes look cheaper.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Survey evidence that respondents may misperceive tax costs versus alternatives (e.g., Jagers & Hammar, 2009).
determinants.evidence_quote: people may overestimate the costs of taxes and underestimate the ‘hidden’ costs of alternative policies

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: distribution of costs is progressive... richer members... pay a larger share and potential revenues are redistributed
determinants.definition: Tax support increases when burdens are progressive and revenues are redistributed to poorer or more vulnerable groups.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived fairness/inequality reduction improves legitimacy of the tax burden and acceptance of paying costs.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Cross-country evidence on domestic climate policy support where progressive distribution and revenue redistribution increase acceptability (e.g., Brannlund & Persson, 2012).
determinants.evidence_quote: more support if the distribution of costs is progressive... richer members... pay a larger share... revenues are redistributed
determinants.notes: Presented as general domestic policy evidence; revenue redistribution is central.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: support for fairness principles ‘equality’ and ‘need’... strongly positive impact on tax support
determinants.definition: Fuel-tax support rises when citizens endorse equality (equal reductions) and need (those who need to emit reduce less) as fairness principles.
determinants.mechanism: Fairness-consistent tax rules increase moral acceptance and perceived equity of emission-reduction burdens.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Swedish attitudes toward fuel taxation mapped to fairness principles (Hammar & Jagers, 2007).
determinants.evidence_quote: ‘equality’ and ‘need’... strongly positive impact on tax support

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: WTP... decrease if general taxpayers are assigned great responsibility... increase if industry and energy users are targeted
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Responsibility assignment: taxpayers vs industry/energy users
determinants.definition: Willingness to pay for carbon/fuel mitigation decreases when general taxpayers are assigned major responsibility rather than industry/energy users.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived burden-sharing shifts personal costs and fairness; public-responsibility framing reduces acceptability and WTP.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: WTP changes based on who is assigned responsibility (Cai, Cameron, & Gerdes, 2010).
determinants.outcome_variable: Willingness to pay for mitigation under tax-like policy framing
determinants.evidence_quote: WTP... decrease if general taxpayers are assigned great responsibility... increase if industry and energy users are targeted

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: acceptability of a carbon tax can vary between 68% and 48% depending on whether revenues are earmarked... or go directly to the general tax budget
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Carbon-tax revenue earmarking vs general budget
determinants.definition: Carbon-tax acceptability is higher when revenues are earmarked for specific purposes rather than allocated to the general tax budget.
determinants.mechanism: Earmarking increases perceived transparency and benefits, improving trust and willingness to support the carbon tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: UK choice experiment: carbon-tax acceptability 68% vs 48% based on earmarking vs general budget (Bristow et al., 2010).
determinants.evidence_quote: acceptability of a carbon tax can vary between 68% and 48% depending on whether revenues are earmarked... or go... to the general tax budget
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 68% acceptability with earmarking vs 48% when revenues go to general tax budget.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Revenue recycling... reducing VAT or income tax — increased policy support
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Gasoline-tax revenue recycling to taxpayers (VAT/income tax reductions)
determinants.definition: Recycling gasoline-tax revenues to citizens via VAT or income-tax reductions increases support for higher gasoline taxation.
determinants.mechanism: Compensation through lower personal taxes offsets perceived gasoline-tax burdens and improves net fairness.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Canada: revenue recycling back to taxpayers increases support for higher gasoline taxes (Hsu, Walters, & Purgas, 2008).
determinants.evidence_quote: Revenue recycling... made a marked difference: reducing VAT or income tax increased policy support

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: support a rise in fossil fuel taxes if revenues are earmarked... whereas without earmarking... prefers a decrease
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Earmarking fossil-fuel tax revenues for environmental purposes
determinants.definition: Support for fossil-fuel tax increases is higher when revenues are earmarked for environmental purposes; without earmarking many prefer tax decreases.
determinants.mechanism: Earmarking links payments to visible environmental benefits, improving legitimacy and perceived effectiveness of the tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Norway choice experiment: support for fossil-fuel tax rises depends on environmental earmarking (Sælen & Kallbekken, 2011).
determinants.outcome_variable: Support/opposition to fossil fuel tax increases
determinants.evidence_quote: would support a rise in fossil fuel taxes if revenues are earmarked for environmental purposes... without earmarking... prefers a decrease

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: policy support requires trust in government and politicians, as exemplified by the case of carbon taxation in Sweden and Norway
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Trust in government/politicians increases carbon-tax support
determinants.definition: Higher trust in government and politicians increases public support for carbon taxation.
determinants.mechanism: Trusted governments are seen as credible implementers and fair managers of carbon-tax burdens and revenues.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon taxation in Sweden and Norway: trust in government/politicians linked to policy support (Hammar & Jagers, 2006; Harring & Jagers, 2013).
determinants.evidence_quote: policy support requires trust in government and politicians... carbon taxation in Sweden and Norway

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: trust in environmental scientists and environmental organizations... trust in industry negatively associated with greater policy support
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Trust in industry reduces carbon/fuel tax support
determinants.definition: Lower trust in industry is associated with reduced policy support for climate taxation (including carbon-tax contexts).
determinants.mechanism: Distrust in industry undermines perceived legitimacy and implementation credibility, lowering support for carbon/fuel taxes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: US study: trust in environmental scientists/NGOs positive; trust in industry negatively associated with greater policy support (Dietz et al., 2007).
determinants.outcome_variable: Policy support for tax-based climate mitigation
determinants.evidence_quote: trust in industry negatively associated with greater policy support
determinants.notes: Policy support is tax-related but carbon-tax instrument is not explicitly named in this sentence.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: support for a carbon tax falls when people receive information that only a minority of other citizens support the proposed tax
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Social norms about others’ support for a carbon tax
determinants.definition: Believing that others support a carbon tax increases its acceptability; learning only minorities support it reduces carbon-tax support.
determinants.mechanism: Normative information affects perceived legitimacy and collective-action expectations, shaping willingness to support the tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Experiment manipulating perceived majority support for a proposed carbon tax (Bolsen, Leeper, & Shapiro, 2013).
determinants.evidence_quote: support for a carbon tax falls when people receive information that only a minority of other citizens support

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: a majority of participants favoured a carbon tax after deliberation
determinants.definition: Public deliberation increases the likelihood that citizens favour a carbon tax.
determinants.mechanism: Deliberation provides additional reasons/arguments and can build consensus, improving acceptance of the carbon tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Australia: study of how public deliberation affects national climate policy preferences; majority favoured a carbon tax after deliberation (Lo et al., 2013).
determinants.evidence_quote: a majority of participants favoured a carbon tax after deliberation

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Republicans preferred to pay more... labelled as a carbon offset than... labelled as a carbon tax (no difference for Democrats)
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Tax-label aversion: carbon tax vs carbon offset wording
determinants.definition: Wording affects WTP for the same charge: Republicans pay more when labelled ‘carbon offset’ rather than ‘carbon tax’; Democrats show no difference.
determinants.mechanism: ‘Tax’ labels trigger negative affect ('tax aversion'); political identity moderates the emotional response.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: US experiment: attribute framing with labels ‘carbon offset’ vs ‘carbon tax’ affects willingness to pay differently by party identification (Hardisty, Johnson, & Weber, 2009).
determinants.outcome_variable: Willingness to pay for the labelled charge
determinants.evidence_quote: Republicans... ‘carbon offset’ than... ‘carbon tax’ (no difference for Democrats)
determinants.notes: Heterogeneous by political identity (Republicans vs Democrats).

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: improved public transport can reduce some public opposition to gasoline (petrol) taxes
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Providing alternatives (public transport) reduces gasoline-tax opposition
determinants.definition: Opposition to gasoline taxes is lower when policies provide alternatives to driving, such as improved public transport.
determinants.mechanism: Alternatives reduce perceived inconvenience/behavioural costs of switching transport behaviour, increasing acceptability of gasoline taxation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian focus group result that improving public transport can reduce opposition to gasoline (petrol) taxes (Kallbekken & Aasen, 2010).
determinants.evidence_quote: improved public transport can reduce some public opposition to gasoline (petrol) taxes

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Deontological concerns resisting a cap-and-trade program
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: Cap-and-trade/ETS permits are excluded per instructions because the determinant is not about a carbon/fuel tax.
